title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202601020H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-04127

CPA HEARING NO. 120,389

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:   January 1, 2019 THROUGH February 28, 2022


Sales And Use Tax/RDT

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


JANICE CAHALANE

Representing Respondent


**************

Representing Petitioner


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on February 23, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a liability.[2]

 

The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.[3]  If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.


SIGNED on this 29th day of January 2026
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT B


 

 

State Office of Administrative Hearings



Kristofer S. Monson



Chief Administrative Law Judge

 

January 14, 2026


 

Janice Cahalane                                                             

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

**************                                                         

For Petitioner

 

RE: SOAH Docket No. 304-26-04127.26; 

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts Hearing No. 120,389

 

Dear Parties:

 

The undersigned issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) regarding the above-referenced matter on December 17, 2025. Petitioner filed exceptions to the PFD with the undersigned on December 29, 2025, and with Staff on January 7, 2026. On January 12, 2026, Staff filed a reply to Petitioner’s exceptions. Petitioner’s exceptions reassert arguments made in the hearing and raise additional taxability contentions not included in the Notice of Hearing, Petitioner’s brief accompanying his exhibits, or the PFD. The scope of a contested case hearing is limited to issues that are included in the Notice of Hearing. See 1 Tex. Admin. Code § 155.401; Tex. Gov’t. Code § 2001.052; Comptroller’s Decision No. 113,393 (2019). Therefore, Petitioner’s request for the consideration of additional contentions is DENIED. Petitioner also requested the opportunity to provide additional documentation. Per 1 Texas Administrative Code § 155.153(10), Petitioner’s request to reopen the record to provide additional documentation is DENIED. Having reviewed the filings of the parties, and finding no errors in the findings of fact or conclusions of law, I recommend that the PFD be adopted as written. Because SOAH has concluded its involvement in the matter, the case is being remanded to the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts. See Tex. Gov’t Code § 2003.051(a).

 

Signed January 14, 2026.

 

Keneshia Washington,                                   

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 


 

ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-26-04127                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 120,389

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) audited ************** (Petitioner) for sales and use tax compliance and made an assessment that included tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested redetermination of the assessment, contending the audit does not account for all tax remitted to the Comptroller, includes nontaxable sales, and assesses erroneously calculated local tax. Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends that the assessment be upheld.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred this case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on October 29, 2025, issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission to Petitioner. On November 5, 2025, ALJ Keneshia Washington issued an Order Setting Written Submission Hearing, setting the hearing requirements, filing deadlines, and the record close date. Janice Cahalane represented Staff, and Petitioner was represented by **************, CPA. The contested case record closed on December 15, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence Presented

 

Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged between the parties prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:


	
	Ninety-Day Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty & Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Package;
	
	
	Audit Documentation Report / Audit Plan; and
	
	
	Letter of Introduction.
	


 

Petitioner offered the following exhibits:


	
	Written Hearing Response;
	
	
	Signed Limited Power of Attorney;
	
	
	Sales and Use Tax Transaction History;
	
	
	Bank Statements for Audit Period;
	
	
	QuickBooks Online (QBO) Sales Tax Report;
	
	
	QBO Report on Purchases;
	
	
	QBO Taxable Sales Detail;
	
	
	Excel Worksheet Bank Statement Deposit Totals;
	
	
	Excel Worksheet Sales & Use Tax Totals;
	
	
	Excel Worksheets Amount Due vs ************** and Corrected Amounts; and
	
	
	Excel Worksheet Percentage Breakdown Gross Sales vs Taxable Sales.
	


 

The parties’ exhibits were admitted to the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

During the period at issue, Petitioner sold, installed, and serviced garage doors for residential and nonresidential customers from his outlet in CITY, Texas. In June 2022, Staff initiated an audit of Petitioner for sales and use tax compliance covering the period of January 1, 2019, through February 28, 2022.

 

Petitioner provided the auditor with available sales invoices for the audit. The auditor used an estimation procedure to conduct the audit because Petitioner provided incomplete records. He used Petitioner’s reported taxable sales for 2015 through 2018 to estimate average yearly sales for that period. He then divided Petitioner’s reported taxable sales for 2015 through 2018 by Petitioner’s taxable sales pursuant to the sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period covering 2015 – 2018 to estimate an audit error percentage. To estimate taxable sales for the period at issue, the auditor applied the estimated audit error percentage to the estimate of average sales yearly sales for gross sales in 2019, as well as the quarterly sales for 2020 and monthly sales for the remainder of the audit period based on Petitioner’s available sales invoices. He then subtracted Petitioner’s taxable sales reported to the Comptroller from the estimated taxable sales to estimate disallowed deductions. The auditor scheduled estimated disallowed deductions scheduled in Exam 1. No purchases were assessed in the audit.

 

The auditor observed that Petitioner reported sales from 2020 as an “outlet filer,” i.e., a taxpayer with all sales consummated at his place of business, instead of a “list filer,” i.e., a taxpayer with sales consummated across various local taxing jurisdictions in Texas. During 2019 and from 2021, Petitioner reported sales as a list filer. Petitioner’s source records, including sales invoices, showed that Petitioner delivered garage doors and performed services outside of the local taxing jurisdictions of his place of business in CITY, Texas. To correct Petitioner’s 2020 filing of local sales tax as an outlet filer, the auditor reallocated Petitioner’s 2020 sales to local jurisdictions using a modification factor based on the reported percentages from Petitioner’s correctly reported periods. The auditor scheduled credit for local taxes paid in 2020 in Exam 3. He then scheduled an estimate of the correct allocation of Petitioner’s sales using the modification factor in Exam 4. The net additional local tax assessed was $0.01.

 

On July 24, 2023, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner. The assessment included tax, a 10% penalty, and interest. Petitioner timely requested redetermination, arguing the audit does not account for all tax remitted to the Comptroller, includes nontaxable sales, and assesses erroneously calculated local tax. Staff did not agree to adjust the audit and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. ALJ’s Analysis and Recommendation

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. For example, tax is imposed on the sale of real property repair and remodeling services. Id. § 151.0101(a)(13). Thus, all persons who remodel nonresidential real property must collect tax on the total sales price to the customer. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(b)(2). However, a person who builds new improvements to realty or who performs residential real property repair and remodeling services is performing nontaxable services and is classified as a contractor rather than as a service provider. Id. § 3.291(a)(3). A contractor is not providing taxable services and is not required to collect sales tax on its labor charges. Id. § 3.291; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,643 (2010). Moreover, all gross receipts of a seller are presumed to have been subject to sales tax unless a properly completed resale or exemption certificate is accepted. Id. § 151.054(a).

 

When tax is imposed on tangible personal property, the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,506 (2020). However, only services specifically enumerated in Texas Tax Code § 151.0101 are taxable. To impose tax on a service, Staff must demonstrate, prima facie, not only that a service was sold but that the service is taxable. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,530 (2015). If that burden is satisfied, Petitioner must demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that tax was assessed on a taxable service in error or, by clear and convincing evidence, that a transaction qualifies for an exemption from tax. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c), (e).

 

The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b). For example, a taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3). If a taxpayer fails to keep accurate records of gross receipts, gross purchases, deductions, and exclusions, the Comptroller may take actions that include estimating the person’s tax liability based on any available information. Tex. Tax Code §§ 111.0042(d), .008(a).

 

In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a). A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012). In addition, a taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,078 (2015). Moreover, the Comptroller has consistently treated missing invoices as 100% taxable. See Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.025, .054; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 39,765 (2003).

 

Based on record evidence, scheduled sales were taxable sales of tangible personal property, e.g., garage doors, or taxable repair and remodeling services, e.g., the commercial repair or installation of garage doors. Staff used an estimation procedure because of Petitioner’s failure to provide complete documentation. Hence, Staff met its prima facie burden to establish that the assessment is comprised of taxable sales based on the best information available.

 

Petitioner contends the audit does not account for all tax remitted to the Comptroller and provides bank statements to support his position. Petitioner also argues that the percentage used in the estimation procedure to assess estimated disallowed deductions in Exam 1 is erroneous. Specifically, he argues that his percentage of taxable sales varies each month and has provided summary schedules to present his proposed percentages of taxable sales. Petitioner also contends that, when calculating the local tax on his sales, the local tax rate should be based on his place of business in CITY, Texas. Petitioner asserts that his calculations and supporting documents show that Petitioner is ultimately due a refund for the period at issue.

 

However, Petitioner’s position regarding the calculation of the local tax applicable to his sales is contradicted by available evidence showing that his sales are consummated across various local taxing jurisdictions across Texas. Hence, compliance with 34 Texas Administrative Code § 3.334 regarding local sales and use taxes requires Petitioner to calculate local sales tax applicable to his sales as a list filer. Furthermore, Petitioner’s bank statements and summary schedules are not sufficient to establish the taxability of assessed sales. The Comptroller has held that summary documents that are created by the taxpayer and submitted without underlying support are tantamount to bare assertions; i.e., they are not considered evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 115,535 (2020), 102,488 (2010). The ALJ concludes Petitioner has not met his burden to establish by clear and convincing evidence that any particular transactions are tax exempt, or to demonstrate audit error by a preponderance of the evidence. Therefore, the ALJ recommends affirming the audit.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	During the period at issue, ************** (Petitioner) sold, installed, and serviced garage doors for residential and nonresidential customers from his outlet in CITY, Texas.
	
	
	In June 2022, The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) initiated an audit of Petitioner for sales and use tax compliance covering the period of January 1, 2019, through  February 28, 2022. No purchases were assessed in the audit. Petitioner provided the auditor with available sales invoices.
	
	
	The auditor used an estimation procedure to conduct the audit because Petitioner provided incomplete records. He used Petitioner’s reported taxable sales for 2015 through 2018 to estimate average yearly sales for that period. He then divided Petitioner’s reported taxable sales for 2015 through 2018 by Petitioner’s taxable sales pursuant to the sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period covering 2015 – 2018 to estimate an audit error percentage.
	
	
	To estimate taxable sales for the period at issue, the auditor applied the estimated audit error percentage to the estimate of average sales yearly sales for gross sales in 2019, as well as the quarterly sales for 2020 and monthly sales for the remainder of the audit period based on Petitioner’s available sales invoices.
	
	
	He then subtracted Petitioner’s taxable sales reported to the Comptroller from the estimated taxable sales to estimate disallowed deductions. The auditor scheduled estimated disallowed deductions in Exam 1.
	
	
	The auditor observed that Petitioner reported sales from 2020 as an “outlet filer,” i.e., a taxpayer with all sales consummated at its place of business, instead of a “list filer,” i.e., a taxpayer with sales consummated across various local taxing jurisdictions in Texas.
	
	
	During 2019 and from 2021, Petitioner reported sales as a list filer. Petitioner’s source records, including sales invoices, showed that Petitioner delivered garage doors and performed services outside of the local taxing jurisdictions of his place of business in CITY, Texas.
	
	
	To correct Petitioner’s 2020 filing of local sales tax as an outlet filer, the auditor reallocated Petitioner’s 2020 sales to local jurisdictions using a modification factor based on the reported percentages from Petitioner’s correctly reported periods.
	
	
	The auditor scheduled credit for local taxes paid in 2020 in Exam 3. He then scheduled an estimate of the correct allocation of Petitioner’s sales using the modification factor in Exam 4. The net additional local tax assessed was $0.01.
	
	
	On July 24, 2023, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner. The assessment included tax, a 10% penalty, and interest.
	
	
	Petitioner timely requested redetermination.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to adjust the audit and referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On October 29, 2025, Staff issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On November 5, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington issued an Order Setting Written Submission Requirements, which set the written submission hearing, filing deadlines, and the record close date.
	
	
	During the SOAH hearing, Petitioner provided documentation to support his contentions.
	
	
	The record closed on December 15, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over these matters. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided a proper and timely notice of hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	Tax is imposed on the sale of real property repair and remodeling services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0101(a)(13).
	
	
	All persons who remodel nonresidential real property must collect tax on the total sales price to the customer. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.357(b)(2).
	
	
	A person who builds new improvements to realty or who performs residential real property repair and remodeling services is performing nontaxable services and is classified as a contractor rather than as a service provider. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.291(a)(3).
	
	
	A contractor is not providing taxable services and is not required to collect sales tax on its labor charges. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.291; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,643 (2010).
	
	
	All gross receipts of a seller are presumed to have been subject to sales tax unless a properly completed resale or exemption certificate is accepted. Tex. Tax Code § 151.054(a).
	
	
	When tax is imposed on tangible personal property, the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,506 (2020).
	
	
	Only services specifically enumerated in Texas Tax Code § 151.0101 are taxable.
	
	
	To impose tax on a service, Staff must demonstrate, prima facie, not only that a service was sold but that the service is taxable. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,530 (2015).
	
	
	If that burden is satisfied, Petitioner must demonstrate by a preponderance of the evidence that tax was assessed on a taxable service in error or, by clear and convincing evidence, that a transaction qualifies for an exemption from tax. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c), (e).
	
	
	The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b).
	
	
	A taxpayer is required to keep records to substantiate each claimed deduction or exclusion authorized by law. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025(a)(3).
	
	
	If a taxpayer fails to keep accurate records of gross receipts, gross purchases, deductions, and exclusions, the Comptroller may take actions that include estimating the person’s tax liability based on any available information. Tex. Tax Code §§ 111.0042(d), .008(a).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing, the taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012).
	
	
	A taxpayer cannot defeat an assessment based on the burden of proof by not providing the necessary documents. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,078 (2015).
	
	
	The Comptroller has consistently treated missing invoices as 100% taxable. See Tex. Tax Code §§ 151.025, .054; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 39,765 (2003).
	
	
	Staff met its prima facie burden to establish that the assessment is comprised of taxable sales based on the best information available.
	
	
	Compliance with 34 Texas Administrative Code § 3.334 regarding local sales and use taxes requires Petitioner to calculate local sales tax applicable to his sales as a list filer.
	
	
	The Comptroller has held that summary documents that are created by the taxpayer and submitted without underlying support are tantamount to bare assertions; i.e., they are not considered evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 115,535 (2020), 102,488 (2010).
	
	
	Petitioner has not met his burden to establish by clear and convincing evidence that any particular transactions are tax exempt, or to demonstrate audit error by a preponderance of the evidence.
	
	
	The ALJ recommends affirming the audit.
	


 

Signed December 17, 2025.

 

ALJ Signature:

 

Keneshia Washington

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 


 

ENDNOTES






[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] At present, insufficient information is available to determine which items and amounts are disputed or undisputed for purposes of Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112. In the absence of this information, the Comptroller will assume the entire amount of the assessment, as it appears in Comptroller’s Decision Attachment A, the Notification of Hearing Results, remains in dispute.

If Petitioner intends to sue the comptroller to dispute an amount of tax, penalty, or interest assessed in a deficiency redetermination or jeopardy determination under Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 111, Petitioner is required to file a motion for rehearing that “states the specific grounds of error and the disputed amounts associated with the grounds of error.” Tex. Tax Code § 112.201(a)(3). Petitioner should refer to Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112, for further guidance regarding a suit after redetermination.



[3] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c).
subjects: ESTIMATED AUDIT-INADEQUATE/MISSING RECORDS/FAILURE TO MAINTAIN RECORDS-COMPTROLLER IS AUTHORIZED TO ESTIMATE BASED ON BEST INFORMATION AVAILABLE (SECTION 111.0042)
